user_input:
Hello HPC support team,

Name: Liam Collins-Jones, Account: lc984, PI: Gemma Bale
I would like to install Toast++ software (http://web4.cs.ucl.ac.uk/research/vis/toast/download_src.html ). I attempted doing this myself towards the start of 2025 but was unsuccessful. Would anyone on your end be able to install this on my account? Happy to provide more details etc.
Thanks! Liam.
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Source Build / User-Space Installation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants help installing Toast++ in their own workspace after an earlier unsuccessful attempt. This looks like a user-space source-build request rather than a request for a central software install. The best initial helpdesk response is to gather any previous build errors and, if needed, attempt a clean user-space build internally before sending tested instructions.

ACTION
1) Confirm the source/version the user is trying to build.
2) Ask for any error output from the user's earlier build attempt if available.
3) If needed, attempt a clean user-space build in `hpc-work` and return tested instructions.
4) Prefer a user-space install unless there is evidence of wider demand for a central package.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Liam,

This looks like a user-space source-build request rather than a central software installation.

If you still have the output from your previous build attempt, please send it to us. If needed, we can also try a clean build internally and then send you tested instructions for building Toast++ in your workspace.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

'source_weights': {'docs': 1.0, 'tickets': 1.0}}, 'text': 'Rapids HBM[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids-hbm "Permalink to this headline")

Since the cpu-r-hbm nodes are running Rocky 9, you will want to recompile
your applications if they are coming from a different operating system or a different OS version.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-sapphire.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-s-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 1:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p ukaea-spr-hbm(-flat) -q INTR
```

to rebuild and re-optimise your applications to get the best from these specialist nodes. The `-q INTR` option used above requests a higher priority interactive jobs, but does not allow for more than 1h wall time. If you need a longer interactive session, you will need to remove the option `-q INTR` from your `sintr` command line and queue for longer.

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

   ```

./configure

Hello,
Is there any chance we can add a module for the last version of blast? I think the last one available is 2.11, and it is not complete (for example blastp is missing)
Thanks.

Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting a newer BLAST module because the currently visible version appears incomplete. The correct initial helpdesk response is to treat this as a software request: check whether a newer supported module already exists and, if not, investigate whether one can be added.

If we are able to add a newer supported version, we will send you the exact module name and load command.

Best wishes,

### Loading, unloading and swapping modules[¶](#loading-unloading-and-swapping-modules "Permalink to this headline")

To load a module to use `module add` or `module load`. For example,
to load the pgi commpiler into the development environment:

```
module load pgi
```

This will load the default version of the pgi commpilers Library. If
you need a specfic version of the module, you can add more information:

```
module load pgi/2018
```

will load version 18.1 for you, regardless of the default. If you
want to clean up, `module remove` will remove a loaded module:

```
module remove pgi
```

```
cd ~
mkdir privatemodules
```

2. Add module files in the `privatemodules` folder
3. Access the private modules with:

```
module load use.own
```

## Compiling[¶](#compiling "Permalink to this headline")

Note that the `default-X` module, which is loaded by default on nodes of type X, arranges for `mpicc`, `mpif90` etc to be found and to use the recommended compilers and MPI implementation automatically when invoked. These wrapper commands supply the correct flags for compiling with the particular MPI implementation to which they belong.

When compiling code, it is usually possible to remove any direct MPI library references in your Makefile as mpicc & mpif90 will take care of these details. In the Makefile, simply set

```
CC=mpicc
```

etc, or define

```
export CC=mpicc
```

etc in the shell before compilation.

If some required libraries are missing, please let us know and we can try to install them centrally (as a module).

## Running[¶](#running "Permalink to this headline")

Please note that the following resource limits apply:

On CPU, SL1 and SL2 users are limited to 4256 cores in use at any one time and a maximum wallclock runtime of 36 hours per job. On GPU, SL1 and SL2 are limited to 64 GPUs in use at any one time and a maximum wallclock runtime of 36 hours per job.
SL3 users are similarly limited to 448 cores (CPU) and 32 GPUs (GPU), all with up to 12 hours per job.
For more information, please see this full description of [service levels (SLs)](/hpc/user-guide/policies.html#service-levels).

user_input:
Dear Support Team,
I’m encountering an issue with Conda environment activation inside Slurm batch jobs on the cluster.
I have a Conda environment named ml that contains its own Python installation (Python 3.10). When I activate it interactively on the login node, it behaves correctly:
- conda activate ml
- which python → points to the env’s interpreter under .../envs/ml/bin/python
- python --version → Python 3.10.4
However, when I submit a Slurm batch job, the job still uses the base Conda Python:
Slurm job:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake
1. Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
2. (adjust to whatever your cluster uses)
module load miniconda
source .bashrc
1. Activate the environment
conda activate ml
1. Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"
1. Alternatively:
2. echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"
1. Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
Output:
Active conda environment: ml
Using Python from: /usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/python
What am I doing wrong here?
Kind regards,
Stefanos
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Conda Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The batch job is falling back to the cluster-wide miniconda Python, even though the named environment appears active. The recommended fix is not to rely on the old shared miniconda module; instead the user should install their own Miniconda into `~/rds/hpc-work` and build environments there.

ACTION
1) Explain that the shared cluster miniconda install is old and can behave unexpectedly.
2) Instruct the user to install their own Miniconda in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Recreate or move the environment there.
4) In job scripts, activate the personal environment rather than the shared module-based one.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stefanos,

The issue is most likely caused by relying on the old shared miniconda install on the cluster. Please install your own Miniconda under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create your environment there instead.

That personal install will give you full control over the environment and should avoid the base-Python fallback you are seeing in Slurm jobs.

Relevant filesystem guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Conda Environment Activation [1][2]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Portal Action Required: No [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The issue is related to activating a Conda environment in a SLURM script [1][2]. The user has tried various methods, including using `conda activate` and `source activate`, but the environment is not being activated correctly [1][2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To activate the Conda environment in the SLURM script, the user should try the following:
1. Load the Miniconda module: `module load miniconda/3` [1]
2. Activate the Conda environment: `conda activate <env_name>` [1][2]
3. Verify that the environment is activated correctly by checking the Python version and package list [1][2]

QUESTIONS TO ASSESS CURRENT STATE
- Can you provide the exact error message or output when running the SLURM script?
- Have you tried running the script on the login node to see if the environment activates correctly?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Dear Support,
I have tried activating my Conda environment in my SLURM script using `conda activate <env_name>`, but it does not seem to be working. I have attached my script and the output for your reference. Please let me know if there are any issues with my script or if there are any additional steps I need to take.
Best regards,
[User]

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- When activating a Conda environment in a SLURM script, make sure to load the correct Miniconda module and activate the environment using the correct command [1][2].
- Be cautious when using `conda init` as it can modify the shell configuration files [1].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-tools/python.html::chunk::0002

### Installing additional Anaconda Python modules[¶](#installing-additional-anaconda-python-modules "Permalink to this headline")

If the central `base` installation does not have a package or module that you require, you
can install this yourself by using *conda environments*.

A conda environemnt is a local copy of the central install that you can then modify
with additional modules/packages or even use different versions of existing
packages.

Full documentation on using conda environments can be found online at [Managing
conda environments](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/user-guide/tasks/manage-environments.html).

Below we show a short example of creating a local Python environment and installing the
*biopython* package.

Once the virtualenv environment is activated, use the normal methods for
downloading and installing Python packages (e.g. pip) and the packages will be
installed into your YOUR\_PYTHON directory, where they will override the

new components should be visible (as long as the virtualenv environment is

### Additional notes[¶](#additional-notes "Permalink to this headline")

If you are not in the location of the filesystem where `YOUR_PYTHON` is
present, you can use a full path instead of a relative path (e.g.
`/home/my-crsid/YOUR_PYTHON)` to activate the virtual env from every location
of the filesystem.

The command `virtualenv` must be done only once to create and initialize the
sandbox. After that, you just have to activate and deactivate accordingly to
your need.

First we run the CPU MSA step on an Icelake node. The -f flag means that we only run the featurisation step:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -A MY_CPU_ACCOUNT
#SBATCH -p icelake
#SBATCH -N 1
#SBATCH --exclusive
#SBATCH -t 04:00:00

# source conda environment
module load miniconda/3
conda activate parafold

DATA=/datasets/public/AlphaFold/data

./run_alphafold.sh \
-d $DATA \
-o output \
-p monomer_ptm \
-i input/mono_set1/GB98.fasta \
-m model_1 \
-f
```

The featurisation step will output feature.pkl and an MSA directory in the output directory. To run a monomer prediction, execute the following command on the GPU:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -A MY_GPU_ACCOUNT
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH -N 1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH -t 02:00:00

# source conda environment
module load miniconda/3
conda activate parafold

DATA=/datasets/public/AlphaFold/data

./run_alphafold.sh \
-d $DATA \
-o output \
-m model_1,model_2,model_3,model_4,model_5 \
-p monomer_ptm \
-i input/mono_set1/GB98.fasta \
-t 1800-01-01
```

```
ln -s /datasets/public/AlphaFold/data/bfd/bfd_metaclust_clu_complete_id30_c90_final_seq.sorted_opt_a3m.ffindex /local/

user_input:
Good morning,
I’d like to request your support as I’ve had issues setting up and using Python environments on CSD3.
Normally I used my own installation of micromamba and had set up environments with numpy, pytorch, etc. However, as of recently, launching python and running `import torch` freezes completely. I submitted a job consisting only of `python -c import torch` and it took over an hour to complete.
I cleaned up my local environment and completely uninstalled micromamba and cleared all caches.
Then, I attempted to follow the steps here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html). However, the simple step of `python -m venv` took over 3 minutes to complete, indicating the problem is to do with python in general. I checked `which python` and it was using `/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/python-3.11.9-nptrdpllnmurec6o5hvu7iwyca5qgajy/bin/python`
Finally, I attempted to use the pre-installed miniconda3 per the instructions here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html). However setting up a simple environment with pandas and pytorch has been running for several hours as of this email.
Can you help me figure out why it’s being so slow and what to do?
Could it be that the problem is with the file system? I attempted `python -m venv` both in my home directory and on hpc-work and it took very long in both cases.
Thanks for your help,
Stelios Boulitsakis Logothetis
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Platform Incident / Filesystem Performance
Technical Action Required: Yes (investigate possible service-side performance issue)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user’s slow Python setup and `import torch` freezes could reflect either a problem in the user environment or a broader shared-filesystem or service issue. Because the slowdown affects multiple environment-creation methods and both home and `hpc-work`, the first response should check for any active service-side problem before focusing only on the user’s setup.

ACTION
1) Check whether there is a broader service issue affecting Python environment creation or shared filesystems.
2) Ask the user to confirm the login node and working path they are using.
3) If no active incident is found, collect the exact commands and timings needed to separate user-environment issues from platform performance issues.
4) Ask the user to retry once those checks have been completed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stelios,

The symptoms you describe may indicate a broader platform performance issue rather than only a mistake in your Python environment setup.

We are checking this on our side. In the meantime, please confirm which login node and working path you are using so that we can investigate further if needed.

Best regards,
HPC Support

The user is experiencing issues with setting up and using Python environments on CSD3, including slow performance and errors when creating virtual environments and installing packages [1][3]. The user has tried using their own installation of micromamba, as well as the pre-installed miniconda3, but is still encountering problems [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Advise the user to try loading the relevant cluster environment and a recent Python module, as described in the documentation [1][5].
2. Suggest creating a new virtual environment using the `python -m venv` command, as outlined in the documentation [1][3].
3. Recommend installing packages using pip, as described in the documentation [1][5].
4. If issues persist, ask the user to provide more details about their environment and the exact errors they are encountering.

QUESTIONS TO ASSEK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please provide the exact error messages you are seeing when trying to create a virtual environment or install packages?
- Have you tried loading the relevant cluster environment and Python module before creating your virtual environment?
- Are you using a specific version of Python or packages that may be causing compatibility issues?

Hello,
Thank you for your email. I have tried loading the relevant cluster environment and Python module, and creating a new virtual environment using the `python -m venv` command. However, I am still experiencing issues with installing packages. The error message I am seeing is [insert error message]. I am using Python 3.11.9 and trying to install the torch and torchvision packages.
Best regards,
[User]

- When using Python environments on CSD3, it is recommended to use the pre-installed miniconda3 and follow the documentation for creating and managing virtual environments [1][3].
- Users should be aware of the potential for compatibility issues when using different versions of Python or packages [2].

## Setup PyTorch on CSD3[¶](#setup-pytorch-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

The python installation will depend on the target partition (CPU or GPU).
To run Pytorch on a CPU partition (cclake, icelake), please do the following:

```
#!/bin/bash

# load the relevant cluster environment and a recent python built for the cluster:
module purge
module load rhel8/default-ccl # recommended for all RHEL8 CPU partitions (cclake, icelake, sapphire)
module load python/3.11.9/gcc/nptrdpll

# create and activate a virtual environment:

python -m venv ./pytorch-cclake-env
source ./pytorch-cclake-env/bin/activate

# install pytorch into your virtual env (instructions from Intel: https://pytorch-extension.intel.com/installation?platform=cpu&version=v2.7.0%2Bcpu&os=linux%2Fwsl2&package=pip)
python -m pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu
python -m pip install intel-extension-for-pytorch
python -m pip install oneccl_bind_pt --extra-index-url https://pytorch-extension.intel.com/release-whl/stable/cpu/us/
```

To run Pytorch on a GPU partition (ampere), please follow the following instructions:

module load python/3.8.11/gcc-9.4.0-yb6rzr6

We have installed virtualenv into the centrally available Python modules and it
is also available for the native Python installed as part of Scientific Linux 7
on CSD3. If you are happy to use the latter (which is version 2.7.5) there is
no need to load a Python module, otherwise, please load the desired Python
module first.

A guide to using virtualenv can be found here : <https://pypi.Python.org/pypi/virtualenv>.

(and if does not exist you must create one first) e.g. YOUR\_PYTHON, of Python
via:

```
virtualenv YOUR_PYTHON
```

## Running a PyTorch program[¶](#running-a-pytorch-program "Permalink to this headline")

PyTorch will be able to run on both CPU and GPU.

CPU submission script:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account MYACCOUNT-CPU
#SBATCH --partition cclake
#SBATCH --nodes 1
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node 1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task 8
#SBATCH --time 00:30:00
#SBATCH -o pytorch.out
#SBATCH -e pytorch.err

# Module setup: cluster environment and recent python.
module purge
module load rhel8/default-ccl

# Activate relevant python virtual environment.
source pytorch-cclake-env/bin/activate

# Let PyTorch use all available CPU cores for OpenMP, MKL and MKL-DNN backends
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK
export MKL_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK

# Edit and replace 'your_script.py' with your own script
python your_script.py
```

GPU submission script:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account MYACCOUNT-GPU
#SBATCH --partition ampere
#SBATCH --nodes 1
#! change to gpu:4 to use all 4 GPU cards on a GPU node.
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH --time 00:30:00
#SBATCH -o pytorch.out
#SBATCH -e pytorch.err

# Module setup: cluster environment and recent python.
module purge
module load rhel8/default-amp

# Activate relevant python virtual environment.
source pytorch-ampere-env/bin/activate

# Edit and replace 'your_script.py' with your own script
python your_script.py

### Installing additional Anaconda Python modules[¶](#installing-additional-anaconda-python-modules "Permalink to this headline")

If the central `base` installation does not have a package or module that you require, you
can install this yourself by using *conda environments*.

A conda environemnt is a local copy of the central install that you can then modify
with additional modules/packages or even use different versions of existing
packages.

Full documentation on using conda environments can be found online at [Managing
conda environments](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/user-guide/tasks/manage-environments.html).

Below we show a short example of creating a local Python environment and installing the
*biopython* package.

```
[user@login-e-1 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
[user@login-e-1 ~]$ conda create -n biopython biopython
Collecting package metadata: done
Solving environment: done

## Package Plan ##

environment location: /home/user/.conda/envs/biopython

Hi,
I am having an issue when debugging my Python code on the gfx nodes: when I try to run simple scripts which should finish in less than 1 second, they sometimes take 10s of seconds or even minutes in some cases to run.
When interrupting one of these scripts (with CTRL-C) the python traceback shows that the process was terminated when trying to import various modules.
The below terminal output from a very simple script where only a single module is imported is shown below:
[cid:a6653bb8-3762-4b4a-9dc4-5d29b74c798f]
As you can see, I ran the exact same command 3 times, with one of them taking substantially longer than the other two.
This issue seems to happen when more than a few minutes has passed since I last ran a script and is starting to become a real pain to my workflow as I when I make changes to code and want to test them I sometimes have to wait minutes for the script to run when it should take ~1 second.
To give some extra details as to my setup; I am using uv package manager with python 3.12.12. UV sets up a python virtual environment (venv) and installs dependencies there as is best practise.
If I had to guess as to what is going on, it seems to be some kind of file system latency where the venv files are normally in some kind of cache so are fast but after a while has passed they are removed and must be retrieved from somewhere much slower. Note that all of the python files and modules are stored in my RDS hpc-work folder.
Would you be able to look into this please? Perhaps there is some quirk of/issue with the lustre filesystem that is causing this?
Many thanks,
Leo

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeing slow Python imports from a virtual environment stored on `hpc-work`. A likely explanation is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem rather than a Python bug, but support should first confirm there is no known wider incident before treating it as normal shared-storage behaviour.

ACTION
1) Explain that `hpc-work` is a shared RDS filesystem and can slow down when heavily loaded.
2) Check whether any cluster-wide incident is currently known.
3) Advise the user that environments on shared storage may show intermittent latency.
4) If the slowdown becomes persistent or widespread, request timings and node details for escalation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Leo,

A likely cause is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem, since your virtual environment is stored in `hpc-work`, but we would first want to rule out any broader service issue.

If no wider incident is present, imports can sometimes be slower when the shared storage is under heavy load.

If this becomes persistent or starts affecting many commands consistently, please send timings and the exact node and location you are testing from and we can investigate further.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

The user is experiencing intermittent slow execution of Python scripts on the gfx nodes, with delays of up to several minutes when importing modules [1]. The issue seems to be related to the loading of Python libraries, and the user has noticed that the problem occurs when more than a few minutes have passed since the last script execution [1]. A similar issue was reported in [7], where the user experienced extremely slow runtime when loading Python libraries.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
Investigate the possibility of a file system latency issue, particularly with the Lustre filesystem [2]. Check if the user's Python virtual environment is properly configured and if the dependencies are correctly installed [1]. Consider suggesting alternative methods for loading Python libraries or optimizing the user's workflow to minimize the impact of the latency issue.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more details about your Python script and the specific libraries that are causing the delay?
- Have you tried running your script on a different node or with a different Python version?
- Are there any other users experiencing similar issues on the gfx nodes?

Best regards,
HPC Support

- Be cautious when investigating file system latency issues, as they can be complex and may require collaboration with the system administrators [2].

Hi,
My name is Huw Cheston. I’m a PhD student at the Centre for Music & Science @ Cambridge, and I’m using the HPC under Peter Harrison’s PI account (`HARRISON-SL3-GPU`). I’m hoping to run some deep learning training jobs on the GPU `ampere` nodes.

The job proceeds through the queue, but errors with the `Illegal instruction` command when trying to run the Python script. All scripts are below:
Bash script:
```shell
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J dpi_train
#SBATCH --account HARRISON-SL3-GPU
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH --nodes 1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#! SBATCH --exclusive
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#SBATCH --mail-user=hwc31@cam.ac.uk
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00


echo "Changing directories..."
cd /home/hwc31/deep-pianist-identification

echo "Activating virtual environment..."
source venv/bin/activate

python tester.py
```
Python `tester.py`
```python
import torch
import mlflow

print("Hello world")
```
I submit the script via `sbatch <bash-script>`. After the job starts, the output of the `slurm` log files looks like:
```
Changing directories...
Activating virtual environment...
/var/spool/slurm/slurmd/job62312614/slurm_script: line 19: 1248137 Illegal instruction     python tester.py
```
so it seems as if the error is occurring when running the Python script, rather than `cd`’ing to the correct directory or activating the `venv`.
Things I have tried:
- Running the same script via `bash` on the login nodes: works
- Removing the `import torch`, `import mlflow` lines and running via `sbatch`: works
- Launching an interactive session on `ampere` via `sintr -A HARRISON-SL3-GPU -p ampere`, `ssh`’ing in to the assigned GPU node, and running the script from there: works (and I am able to run full training scripts this way, too).
- Running the same script but replacing the `python tester.py` with `pip freeze`: works, and all environment dependencies seem to be installed (`torch` and `mlflow` are present, etc.)
I will also try running the same script and redirecting the Python output via `python tester.py &> py_output.log`.
Any advice you might be able to offer would be appreciated! Thanks,

Good afternoon!
I emailing regarding a persistent issue I am having in running a Snakemake workflow on the HPC. I created the workflow on my local machine and it contains a series of python scripts which have been working successfully locally both outside of the workflow and when running it as a whole. The individual scripts are also working on the HPC via normal sbatch submissions (I have been using cclake so far). The conda environment I am using on the HPC has been created from a .txt file of the environment I am using locally.
However, when I run the workflow using snakemake --profile {profile_name} it crashes when executing one of the rules with the script: option. The problem seems to be when using a python package pybedtools(0.8.1), which is a python wrapper of bedtools. Outside of slurm jobs created by the snakemake workflow, this works fine and the function I am accessing from pybedtools successfully calls the bedtools command line and it returns an usable object further down the script. The Singularity image was built from Deepmind’s docker script and has been tested on the A100 nodes. The MSA construction and model inference are done on the same node type - it isn’t easy to decouple the two steps without using separate implementations (see below). Users can choose to run on CPU but the inference step takes considerably longer than on a GPU. Running on a GPU means that the CPU preprocessing (MSA) step can dominate the running time (depending on the particular sequence we wish to predict the structure of).

## Current Issues[¶](#current-issues "Permalink to this headline")

We are aware of the slow preprocessing time of hhblits on CSD3 and are working to improve this. For the small database it’s possible to pre-stage the data on the local ssd drive (with rsync), but this is not possible for the full database as it exceeds the capacity of the local ssd drive.

Dear support team,
I am trying to log in to my RCS storage (rcs-cez22-zielinski) using FileZilla. I am following the instructions given in "Reseach Storage Documentation”. Unfortunately, I am denied access after entering my password and the TOTP verification code (see screenshot).
I can succesfully log in to [https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/](https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/), so I assume I have the correct password. I have also succesfully set up MFA/TOTP for CSD3 (see screenshot), and I am correctly entering the code when prompted to do so.
Maybe I need a different password that is specific for this service?
Kind regards
Christina
Prof. Dr. med. Christina Zielinski
Professor of Infection and Immunity
Department of Pathology
University of Cambridge
Tennis Court Road
Cambridge CB2 1QP
cez22@cam.ac.uk

[IMG_5398.PNG]
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RCS Access / Group Membership / MFA
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The access failure is unlikely to be explained by a separate service-specific password, especially since the user can log in elsewhere successfully. The most likely next steps are to check whether there is an access-control/group-membership problem and to confirm that the user has generated the correct MFA token for RCS/CSD3 access via `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

ACTION
1) Check whether the account has the correct storage/access-group membership.
2) Instruct the user to create the correct TOTP via:
   `ssh <crsid>@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
3) Share the MFA documentation.
4) Remind the user of the normal login path and storage path once access has been confirmed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Christina,

You should not need a separate password for this service. We will check your storage access permissions on our side.

You will also need to create the MFA token that works with RCS/CSD3 access. Please run:
`ssh cez22@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the on-screen instructions. Guidance is here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk

Best wishes,
HPC Support

To resolve this issue, we recommend that the user follows the instructions on the CSD3 website to set up MFA [5]. Specifically, the user should log in to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk and follow the prompts to set up TOTP [5]. The user should then try accessing their RCS storage using the new TOTP code [4]. If the issue persists, the user should try accessing their RCS storage using the command line interface, as described in the documentation [4].

QUESTIONS TO ASSESS (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm that you have set up MFA correctly, as described in the documentation [5]?
- Have you tried accessing your RCS storage using the command line interface, as described in the documentation [4]?

If you are still experiencing issues, please try accessing your RCS storage using the command line interface, as described in the documentation [4]. If you have any further questions or concerns, please do not hesitate to contact us.
Best regards,
HPC Support

### Step 2: Log into multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk[¶](#step-2-log-into-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk "Permalink to this headline")

Once you have connected, you may see a password prompt to log in.

![multi login - access](../_images/multi-login.png)

**University of Cambridge users**: You will need to enter your University password.

**External users**: You will need to use your SSH key or your password, depending on how your account was created.

If TOTP is already configured for your account, a message will be printed and the connection closed:

![multi login - TOTP already configured](../_images/multi-noop.png)

Otherwise, a QR code and secret key will be printed.

![multi login - QR generated](../_images/multi-qr.png)

*Please be careful not to point your camera at the example QR code displayed on the web page, as it will happily read it but this code is not valid for any purpose. You want to use* **your** *QR code from* **your** *login session to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk.*

### Step 3: Scan the QR code or enter the secret key.[¶](#step-3-scan-the-qr-code-or-enter-the-secret-key "Permalink to this headline")

Mandatory MultiFactor Authentication (MFA) was introduced to CSD3 and storage services logins on 1st November 2022 (you can read the archived [full maintenance here](/hpc/full_maintenance-Nov2022.html)).

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are having trouble setting up your MFA, please consult the [FAQs](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#frequently-asked-questions-faq) or watch our [video tutorial](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OvTgniwVxEg&t=365s)

## What does this mean?[¶](#what-does-this-mean "Permalink to this headline")

As well as your passwords and SSH keys, when you use the services, a *Time-based One-Time Password* (TOTP) code will be requested. This is a six digit number, refreshed every 30 seconds, which is generated typically by an app running on your mobile phone. Thus authentication will then require at least two “factors”, i.e. either password or SSH key (the first factor), plus TOTP (the second factor).

Two common such mobile apps (available from mobile app stores and tested by us) are *Google Authenticator* and *Microsoft Authenticator*, which indeed you may already have and be using for other services.

## Walkthrough - Login-Web[¶](#walkthrough-login-web "Permalink to this headline")

Setting up your MFA for login-web is a simple process.
Whether you are logging in for the first time or after getting your MFA reset, you will need to visit <https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk>, enter your CSD3 credentials to login.
You will then be presented with the QR code that you can scan into your authentication app.
The correct TOTP created in this way will appear in your mobile app under the account **CSD3: your username**.

## Will I need to change my working practices?[¶](#will-i-need-to-change-my-working-practices "Permalink to this headline")

People work in different ways, and the answer to this is “possibly”.

user_input:
Good evening,
I have recently been trying to connect to the CSD3 to start working in the cluster for the first time. I have Two-Factor Authentication on my account already enabled (please see image attached).
When I try to connect to the login node (login.hpc.cam.ac.uk) through ssh I am being requested to provide the TOTP Verification Code. Unfortunately, I did not retain the QR code when connecting for the first time to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Would it be possible to reset this multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk QR code so I can scan it with Google Authenticator to set the login for my account?
Many thanks for your assistance on this matter.
Martin
________________________________
From: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Sent: 27 January 2026 14:44
To: Martin Santamarina Garcia <ms3242@cam.ac.uk>
Subject: CSD3 systems at Cambridge (ms3242)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: MFA Reset / SSH Re-enrolment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has lost access to the TOTP setup previously generated for cluster SSH login and needs the standard cluster MFA reset/re-enrolment path. After reset, they should SSH to `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk` again to obtain a new QR code for cluster login.

ACTION
1) Confirm whether the user can transfer the existing authenticator setup from the old device; if not, follow the standard cluster MFA reset workflow.
2) Reset the user’s cluster MFA using the normal support process.
3) Instruct them to run:
   `ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
   using their Cambridge password to obtain a new QR code.
4) Clarify that the web interface may prompt separately if web MFA also needs re-enrolment.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Martin,

We can reset the MFA for your cluster account and then you can re-enrol it.

Once the reset has been completed, please run:
`ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the prompts to scan the new QR code into your authenticator app.

Best regards,
HPC Support

CLASSIFICATION
Category: HPC / MFA Setup
Technical Action Required: Yes
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is having trouble setting up their TOTP MFA secret for their phone after attempting to connect to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk [3]. They are unable to scan the QR code or manually enter the secret key, resulting in an "invalid key" error [3]. 

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, the user should try the following steps:
1. Ensure they are using the correct password when connecting to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk [7].
2. If the issue persists, try logging in via login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk, which will also prompt for MFA setup [7].
3. If the user is still unable to set up their MFA, they should contact the helpdesk for further assistance.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Are you using the correct password when connecting to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk?
- Have you tried logging in via login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To resolve the issue with setting up your TOTP MFA secret, please try the following steps:
1. Ensure you are using the correct password when connecting to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
2. If the issue persists, try logging in via login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk, which will also prompt for MFA setup.
If you are still unable to set up your MFA, please contact the helpdesk for further assistance.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Users must use their University password or SSH key to log in to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk [3].
- TOTP MFA secrets should be kept secure and not shared with anyone [8].

Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Ctp534%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C5d7a9a5a88ca442db08d08de0bcd7087%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638961174139092243%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=Qc4y52LSdf22c4ceQnF63kp0s9um5LIMJwZw3XVae20%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Ctp534%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C5d7a9a5a88ca442db08d08de0bcd7087%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638961174139092243%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=Qc4y52LSdf22c4ceQnF63kp0s9um5LIMJwZw3XVae20%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,

**Cascade Lake/Ice Lake/Sapphire Rapids**

The cclake-\* partitions assign usage in units of CPU core hours. By convention projects containing CPU core hours have names ending in -CPU.

Jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.

```
#SBATCH -p cclake
```

```
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
```

In addition to *cclake* there are now also *icelake* and *sapphire* partitions which work in a similar way. The precise numbers of CPUs per node and mount of memory available per CPU differ across these types since the hardware differs. Please see the directory

for template submission scripts appropriate to each of these node types (including for GPU).

Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
Peta4-CPU\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0 - Intel Skylake/Omni-Path, Cascade Lake/IB
\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0 and Ice Lake/IB CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU\xa0\xa0 - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR IB GPU cluster.
Please note that currently:
\xa0 Skylake and Cascade Lake use a RHEL7-compatible O/S
\xa0 Ice Lake and Wilkes3 use a RHEL8-compatible O/S
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
This combination of RHEL7 and RHEL8 will become pure RHEL8 in time.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk 
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to either of the addresses below:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk will take you to one of a set of RHEL7,
OPA-enabled login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development
work for the RHEL7 Peta4 clusters. login-icelake will take you to a
different set of RHEL8, Mellanox OFED and CUDA-enabled login nodes
best suited for development work for Ice Lake and Wilkes3-GPU.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
\xa0\xa0\xa0 MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
\xa0\xa0\xa0 SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
\xa0\xa0\xa0 MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
\xa0\xa0\xa0 SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best
snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a mixture of RHEL7 and RHEL8-compatible
operating systems. We recommend that all application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on skylake* and cclake* partitions, please use
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk (aka login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk).
For development on icelake* and ampere* partitions, please use
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk. 
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see 
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. The module names contain hashes uniquely
identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be
searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for
modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing
builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C01%7Cbw450%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C86abf562d39b49259de308daf7c9e82b%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638094743795512858%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=5Y5YvT%2BehC1Fw7mVs32Zt5jXi%2BZmpS3zbZb1xQfYoWk%3D&reserved=0.)](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C01%7Cbw450%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C86abf562d39b49259de308daf7c9e82b%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638094743795512858%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=5Y5YvT%2BehC1Fw7mVs32Zt5jXi%2BZmpS3zbZb1xQfYoWk%3D&reserved=0.))
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses SLURM for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU 
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
 
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
SLURM submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Skylake
-------
 
Skylake jobs require the partitions skylake or skylake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p skylake
or
#SBATCH -p skylake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
skylake partition provides 1 cpu and 6020MiB of RAM per task, and the
skylake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 12060MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 6020MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the Skylake nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 32 cores in total.
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake
-------------------------
 
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake partitions work in a similar way with the following
differences:
The cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3420MiB per task by default.
The cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task by default.
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3380MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
\xa0
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
 
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=\xa0 N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
2240 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,

Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cmg445%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C7272f96fc73045ba3cd708dda7447032%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638850634605886593%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=MTk%2FGc74xAlBy4VIXpkRfojLSJtkd8BT6UM4AlfN0II%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cmg445%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C7272f96fc73045ba3cd708dda7447032%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638850634605886593%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=MTk%2FGc74xAlBy4VIXpkRfojLSJtkd8BT6UM4AlfN0II%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
